abstract: A central challenge in electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction is the development of catalytic materials that combine high product selectivity and long-term stability with a composition of earth-abundant elements. Here, we present thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as atomically defined, nanostructured catalysts capable of the efficient and selective reduction of $CO_2$ to CO in aqueous electrolytes. Investigating a cobalt-porphyrin MOF, $Al_2(OH)_2TCPP-Co$, we demonstrate a selectivity for CO production exceeding 76% and sustained stability over 7 hours, achieving a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical analysis offered a real-time window into the cobalt oxidation states, revealing that most catalytic centers within the MOF are redox-accessible and that the reduction of Co(II) to Co(I) drives the catalytic cycle.
